sections.heading: National reported intimate partner violence was lower in 2025 than in 2022
sections.body: Nationally, 15.4 percent of women who ever had a husband or intimate partner reported physical, sexual, or emotional violence in 2025, down from 17.5 percent in 2022. The decline appears across the component measures: emotional violence fell from 15.2 to 14.0 percent, physical violence from 6.4 to 5.3 percent, sexual violence from 2.3 to 1.8 percent, and physical or sexual violence from 7.3 to 5.8 percent. The overlap measures changed less, with physical and sexual violence at 1.3 percent and all three forms together at 1.2 percent in 2025.

sections.heading: Regional levels varied widely, with Caraga and Bicol at the high end
sections.body: The regional spread in 2025 was large: physical, sexual, or emotional violence ranged from 24.0 percent in Caraga to 3.2 percent in BARMM. Bicol Region was nearly as high as Caraga at 23.9 percent, followed by Zamboanga Peninsula¹ at 21.1 percent, Central Luzon at 20.5 percent, and Eastern Visayas at 20.3 percent. These higher-rate regions were not identical in their component profiles. Caraga had the highest emotional violence level among regions at 21.8 percent, while Bicol had the highest physical violence level at 9.1 percent and the highest physical or sexual violence level at 10.2 percent.

sections.heading: Some regions improved sharply while others moved upward
sections.body: Several regions had lower 2025 levels than in 2022, but the changes were uneven. Ilocos Region fell from 25.0 to 12.8 percent on physical, sexual, or emotional violence, and Central Visayas fell from 18.6 to 9.2 percent. MIMAROPA also declined from 23.7 to 19.5 percent, and Davao Region from 20.7 to 16.9 percent. In contrast, Caraga rose from 20.2 to 24.0 percent and Central Luzon rose from 17.0 to 20.5 percent, showing that the national decline did not apply uniformly across regions.

sections.heading: Physical and sexual violence did not always track the summary measure
sections.body: The regions with high physical, sexual, or emotional violence were often high because of emotional violence, but physical and sexual violence added a different layer. Davao Region had a lower summary level than Caraga, Bicol, Zamboanga Peninsula, Central Luzon, Eastern Visayas, MIMAROPA, and Western Visayas, yet it had one of the highest 2025 physical or sexual violence levels at 9.3 percent. Western Visayas was also elevated on physical or sexual violence at 8.6 percent, with sexual violence at 3.1 percent. The pattern suggests that regional risk cannot be read from the summary measure alone.

sections.heading: BARMM remained the lowest regional outlier across all components
sections.body: BARMM was the clear low outlier in 2025, with 3.2 percent reporting physical, sexual, or emotional violence. Its component values were also the lowest or among the lowest: 2.9 percent for emotional violence, 0.7 percent for physical violence, 0.4 percent for sexual violence, and 0.9 percent for physical or sexual violence. The region also declined from 5.6 percent in 2022 on the summary measure, but the data alone do not explain why its profile is so different from the rest of the country.

sections.heading: Age differences were modest, with the highest summary levels in the late twenties and forties
sections.body: Across age groups, 2025 physical, sexual, or emotional violence stayed within a narrow band, from 14.3 percent among women aged 20 to 24 to 16.1 percent among women aged 40 to 49. Women aged 25 to 29 were close behind at 16.0 percent, and adolescents aged 15 to 19 were at 15.4 percent. Physical violence rose from 3.3 percent among ages 15 to 19 to 6.3 percent among ages 25 to 29, then stayed near 5 percent in the older age groups. The age pattern is therefore present, but less pronounced than the regional, marital status, and wealth divides.

sections.heading: Religion categories showed large contrasts, but some high values came from small groups
sections.body: Among religion categories with 2025 values, Islam had the lowest physical, sexual, or emotional violence level at 7.1 percent, while Roman Catholic women were at 15.6 percent, Protestant women at 17.1 percent, and Iglesia ni Cristo women at 14.7 percent. Aglipay was high at 23.1 percent, driven especially by physical violence at 13.7 percent, while the Other category reached 24.3 percent, almost entirely because emotional violence was 24.2 percent. Because Aglipay and Other had much smaller 2025 sample counts than the largest religion categories, those higher values should be read with more caution than the broad national pattern.

sections.heading: Ethnic group differences were substantial, with high values among Bikolano and Waray women
sections.body: Bikolano and Waray women had the highest unflagged 2025 levels of physical, sexual, or emotional violence among ethnic groups, at 22.2 and 21.4 percent. Both were driven mainly by emotional violence, at 20.0 percent in each group, but Bikolano women also had comparatively high physical violence at 8.4 percent and physical or sexual violence at 9.1 percent. Maranao women had the lowest unflagged summary level at 5.7 percent. Aeta values were higher but flagged in the display data, so they should not be treated as a stable ranking point.

sections.heading: The rural-urban gap narrowed, but rural women still reported higher physical and sexual violence
sections.body: Rural women reported 15.9 percent physical, sexual, or emotional violence in 2025, compared with 15.0 percent among urban women. The gap was much smaller than in 2022, when the corresponding values were 19.9 and 15.6 percent. Rural women still had higher 2025 levels for physical violence at 5.8 percent versus 4.9 percent, sexual violence at 2.2 percent versus 1.5 percent, and physical or sexual violence at 6.4 percent versus 5.3 percent. Emotional violence was closer, at 14.3 percent in rural areas and 13.8 percent in urban areas.

sections.heading: Marital status was the strongest divide in the page data
sections.body: Divorced, separated, or widowed women had by far the highest 2025 levels: 41.4 percent reported physical, sexual, or emotional violence, 39.1 percent emotional violence, 17.3 percent physical violence, 6.7 percent sexual violence, and 18.3 percent physical or sexual violence. Women who had an intimate partner but did not currently have one were also high on the summary measure at 33.4 percent. By comparison, married or living-together women were at 13.9 percent, never-married women at 15.7 percent, and women who currently had an intimate partner at 7.4 percent. The marital status gradient is much sharper than the age or residence pattern.

sections.heading: Employment status showed convergence in 2025
sections.body: Employment groups looked more similar in 2025 than in 2022. Physical, sexual, or emotional violence was 15.4 percent among women employed for cash, 15.2 percent among women employed not for cash, and 15.5 percent among women not employed. This is a notable change from 2022, when women employed not for cash had the highest level at 22.1 percent. Component values still differed somewhat in 2025: physical or sexual violence was 6.3 percent among women employed for cash, 5.5 percent among women not employed, and 4.4 percent among women employed not for cash.

sections.heading: Education showed a broad gradient, but not a perfectly linear one
sections.body: Women with college education had the lowest 2025 physical, sexual, or emotional violence level by education group at 11.4 percent, with physical or sexual violence at 3.2 percent. Women with Grades 1 to 6 education were highest on the summary measure at 20.2 percent, followed by Grades 11 to 12 at 19.1 percent and Grades 7 to 10 at 17.7 percent. The no-education group had 12.7 percent on the summary measure but much higher physical violence at 9.4 percent and sexual violence at 4.3 percent, based on a smaller sample count. The broad pattern links higher schooling, especially college, with lower reported violence, but the middle education categories do not form a simple step-by-step decline.

sections.heading: Wealth remained a clear socioeconomic gradient
sections.body: The wealth pattern was one of the clearest gradients in the data. In 2025, physical, sexual, or emotional violence was 19.1 percent in the lowest wealth quintile, 16.5 percent in the second, 15.7 percent in the middle, 15.4 percent in the fourth, and 10.6 percent in the highest. Physical or sexual violence also declined from 8.2 percent in the lowest quintile to 2.8 percent in the highest, and physical violence from 7.4 to 2.4 percent. Every wealth quintile was lower on the summary measure than in 2022, but the 2025 gap between the lowest and highest quintiles remained substantial.

takeaways: National reported intimate partner violence declined from 2022 to 2025, but 15.4 percent of women who ever had a husband or intimate partner still reported physical, sexual, or emotional violence.
Regional differences were large, with Caraga and Bicol Region near one-quarter on the summary measure and BARMM far lower at 3.2 percent.
Marital status was the sharpest divide, with divorced, separated, or widowed women reporting much higher violence than other marital status groups.
Wealth and education gradients were clear: the highest wealth quintile and college-educated women had the lowest levels in their respective groups.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
